original_hypothesis.title: SwapAndBridgeHandler Has No Function to Remove Eligible Users, Creating Irrevocable Access
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The SwapAndBridgeHandler contract has addEligibleUser but no corresponding removeEligibleUser function. Once an address is granted swap/bridge execution rights via addEligibleUser, this access can never be revoked. If a connector is decommissioned or compromised, its swap/bridge privileges persist indefinitely.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The isEligibleToUse mapping is only set to true via addEligibleUser and the constructor. There is no setter to false. All connector addresses added as eligible users retain permanent access.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function addEligibleUser(address _user) external onlyMaintainerOrEmergency { isEligibleToUse[_user] = true; } — no corresponding remove function exists

preconditions: An address has been added as eligible user
The address needs to be revoked (decommissioned connector, compromised address)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Decommissioned connectors retain swap/bridge execution privileges
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Maintainer/emergency cannot revoke eligible user status

operational_questions: How many eligible users have been added in production?
Are any eligible users decommissioned connectors?
Can an eligible user abuse swap/bridge access after being decommissioned?
validation_requirements: Test that a decommissioned connector can still execute swaps

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Eligible user address is decommissioned or compromised
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute unauthorized swaps or bridges using retained privileges
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No administrative mechanism to revoke access
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Access to a previously eligible address

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After eligible user should be revoked

validation_result.impact_summary: Once an address is granted eligible user status via addEligibleUser, there is no mechanism to revoke it. A decommissioned or compromised connector retains permanent executeSwap/executeBridge privileges.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The GenericSwapAndBridgeHandler contract contains an `addEligibleUser` function that sets `isEligibleToUse[_user] = true`, but there is no corresponding function to set this mapping to `false`. The `isEligibleToUse` mapping is only ever written to `true` — in the constructor loop and in `addEligibleUser`. The `onlyEligibleUser` modifier gates both `executeSwap` and `executeBridge`, meaning any address once granted eligibility retains permanent swap/bridge execution rights. This is inconsistent with other access control patterns in the same codebase: `addConnector` in Registry.sol accepts a `bool[] _enableds` parameter allowing both enable and disable, `addHandler` in LifiImplementation.sol accepts a `bool state` parameter, and `setEnableRoute` can toggle route status. The absence of a removal mechanism for eligible users is a clear code defect.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. GenericSwapAndBridgeHandler.sol lines 65-68 show the `addEligibleUser` function that only sets `isEligibleToUse` to `true`. A comprehensive search of the contract confirms no function exists to set this mapping to `false`. The mapping declaration at line 13 (`mapping(address => bool) public isEligibleToUse`) and all writes to it (constructor lines 39-41 and addEligibleUser line 66) only ever assign `true`.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This function only sets isEligibleToUse to true. No corresponding removeEligibleUser function exists anywhere in the contract, making eligibility irrevocable.

vulnerable_code_snippets.function_name: isEligibleToUse mapping declaration and constructor initialization
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The mapping is only ever written to true in the constructor and addEligibleUser. No code path sets it to false.
This modifier gates executeSwap and executeBridge. Since isEligibleToUse can never be set to false, once-eligible addresses permanently bypass this check.

calling_sequence: 1. Maintainer/Emergency calls addEligibleUser(_user) to grant access
3. _user can now call executeSwap and executeBridge via onlyEligibleUser modifier
4. If _user needs to be revoked (compromised/decommissioned), no function exists to set isEligibleToUse[_user] = false
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The complete contract GenericSwapAndBridgeHandler.sol was searched for any write to `isEligibleToUse` that sets it to `false`. Only two code paths write to this mapping: the constructor at lines 39-41 (sets to `true`) and `addEligibleUser` at line 66 (sets to `true`). No function, modifier, or code path sets any entry to `false`. The `executeSwap` (line 76) and `executeBridge` (line 108) functions both use the `onlyEligibleUser` modifier (line 27), which checks `isEligibleToUse[msg.sender]`. Since this can never become false for an address once set, the access is permanent.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deploySharedContracts.ts script shows SwapAndBridgeHandler is deployed with an empty array for usersAddresses: `const swapHandler = await SwapHandler.deploy([], valueOracle, registryAddress, vaultId);`. This confirms eligible users are added post-deployment via addEligibleUser, making the missing removal function relevant for operational use.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid: (1) A connector address is added as eligible user via addEligibleUser. (2) The connector is later decommissioned (disabled in registry via addConnector with enabled=false). (3) Despite being disabled in the registry, the connector's address retains isEligibleToUse=true status. (4) The decommissioned address can still call executeSwap and executeBridge on the SwapAndBridgeHandler. Note that the executeSwap/executeBridge functions themselves don't check if the caller is an active connector - they only check isEligibleToUse. This means a compromised eligible address could trigger swaps/bridges even after being disabled in the registry. The only mitigation would be contract redeployment or if a new SwapAndBridgeHandler is deployed and the old one is no longer referenced.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity is appropriate. The defect means: (1) Admin cannot revoke swap/bridge execution rights from any address. (2) A compromised or decommissioned connector retains the ability to call executeSwap and executeBridge. (3) The only workaround is deploying a new SwapAndBridgeHandler contract and reconfiguring all connectors. This is inconsistent with the broader codebase pattern where similar boolean mappings support bidirectional updates (e.g., LifiImplementation.addHandler, Registry.addConnector).
recommended_mitigations: Add a removeEligibleUser function: `function removeEligibleUser(address _user) external onlyMaintainerOrEmergency { isEligibleToUse[_user] = false; }`

Alternatively, modify addEligibleUser to accept a bool parameter: `function setEligibleUser(address _user, bool eligible) external onlyMaintainerOrEmergency { isEligibleToUse[_user] = eligible; }` — consistent with patterns used in LifiImplementation.addHandler and Registry.addConnector
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy SwapAndBridgeHandler with empty usersAddresses. 2. Call addEligibleUser(connectorA) as maintainer. 3. Verify connectorA can call executeSwap. 4. Search entire contract ABI for any function that could set isEligibleToUse[connectorA] = false. 5. Confirm no such function exists. 6. Attempt to simulate disabling connectorA's access — confirm it cannot be done.
uncertainties: While the code defect is confirmed, the practical impact depends on whether eligible users are always connectors (which have separate enable/disable in Registry) or could be arbitrary addresses
A workaround exists: deploying a new SwapAndBridgeHandler contract and updating all connector references, though this is operationally expensive
additional_testing_needed: Verify whether eligible users in production are connector addresses or other types of addresses
Test that a disabled connector (in Registry) that is still an eligible user in SwapAndBridgeHandler can successfully execute swaps/bridges
Verify the interaction between isEligibleToUse and the connector-level swapHandler reference to understand the full attack surface
